title: Access, ID, and Refresh Tokens
description:
## The Three Tokens Keycloak Issues

After a successful login, Keycloak's token endpoint typically returns **three** tokens. Knowing which one to use where is essential.

| Token | Audience | Purpose | Typical lifespan |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Access token** | Resource servers / APIs | **Authorize API calls** — carries roles & scopes. Usually a signed **JWT**. Sent as `Authorization: Bearer <token>`. | Short (e.g. ~5 min) |
| **ID token** | The **client app** | **Proves authentication** — identity claims (`sub`, `email`, `name`). Not meant for API authorization. | Short |
| **Refresh token** | Keycloak's token endpoint | **Get new access tokens** without forcing the user to log in again. | Longer (bounded by session limits) |

> **Common mistake:** sending the **ID token** to an API. APIs should validate the **access token**. The ID token exists to tell *the client* who logged in.

### What a decoded access-token JWT looks like

A JWT has three dot-separated parts (`header.payload.signature`). The **payload** is just base64url-encoded JSON:

```json
{
  "iss": "https://sso.example.com/realms/acme",
  "aud": "orders-api",
  "sub": "f9a3...e21",
  "exp": 1718700000,
  "iat": 1718699700,
  "azp": "web-store",
  "scope": "openid profile orders:read",
  "realm_access": { "roles": ["support-agent"] },
  "resource_access": { "orders-api": { "roles": ["orders.read"] } },
  "preferred_username": "asmith"
}
```

### How a resource server validates an access token

1. **Signature** — verify the JWT signature against the realm's public keys from **JWKS** (`jwks_uri`). Keys rotate, so fetch/cache them rather than hard-coding.
2. **`exp`** — reject expired tokens (short lifespan limits exposure if leaked).
3. **`iss`** — must equal the expected realm issuer.
4. **`aud` / `azp`** — confirm the token was intended for *this* API.
5. **Roles/scopes** — finally, check the caller has the permission the endpoint requires.

> Because access tokens are short-lived, the client silently uses the **refresh token** to obtain fresh ones in the background — until the SSO session reaches its maximum lifetime or is revoked.
